metadata.from: Bob (Robert Hanifen) — signed 'Your Loving Son, Bob'
metadata.location_written_from: Military base, likely near Alvin (Texas) or Leavenworth area based on context
people_mentioned: Jim (likely brother James Edward Hanifen Jr. — mentioned receiving a letter from him)
metadata.mood_tone: Informative and conversational; generally upbeat despite minor health issues; practical concerns about furlough travel costs and a missing raincoat
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words require interpretation; ink is faded in places; 'Dean' could be a name or another word; 'tonsellites' is a misspelling of tonsillitis; some words at line endings are partially cut off or smudged
metadata.historical_context: Letter appears to be from WWII era military training; references to furloughs, basic training, Army Corps (Air Corps likely), Captain briefing soldiers, and travel reimbursement policies suggest wartime military service context. Reference to all men in the Air Corps seeing the Captain and being told about furloughs before December suggests wartime mobilization.
metadata.ambiguity_notes: 'Jim' referenced as someone who sent a letter and hasn't been warm for two days — context (receiving letters, weather comments) suggests this is brother Jim Jr. rather than the father, since the letter is addressed to Dad.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob mentions a furlough policy for 18-year-olds under December 1 — getting a 14-day furlough after their current term — and discusses travel pay between home, Des Moines, and Leavenworth, then to 'basic right from here.' He is restricted but planning to go to Alvin (likely Alvin, Texas, near Sheppard Field or a similar Southern base). References a letter from Jimmy saying he 'hasn't been warm for two days' (cold weather, suggesting fall), warm weather currently. This fits the late summer/fall 1944 period at the University of Wyoming or a transition base. The travel pay from Des Moines to Leavenworth (used in Letter 18a for the Fort Leavenworth to Laramie movement) and the 14-day furlough policy for 18-year-olds aligns with the ASTP/Air Corps training context of 1944. Estimated September–October 1944.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad

I got your letter and the money. I haven't been down to Alvin lately because I have restricted but I am going down this week end if they we get off in time. Got another letter from Jimmy Saturday and he says he hasn't been warm for two days. It was warm here yesterday and today. We haven't wore nothing but shirt sleeve for a couple of days. All men in the Air Corps saw the Captain Sat. and he explains to us what was going on. All 18 yr. before Dec. 1 will receive a 14 day furlough if they wish to after this term is finished. You can go to basic right from here if you want to, but everybody wants a furlough. The govt. pays our way from our home to Leavenworth, but we have to pay our way home from here. If we leave for basic training from here they will pay our way

there. I will make enough from Des Moines to Leavenworth to make up for my fare from Laramie to Des Moines. I called up Sunday about 7:15 and it took a few min to get thru and I am sorry I missed getting to talk to you. I hope Dean had a nice birthday. We have been playing basketball in P.T. lately and between platoon. Yesterday was the first day I seem to get the feel of the ball and I am made a few points. I have been feeling pretty good lately but the last few days my stomach has been about halfway upset but I still am walking. My roommate has been in the infirmary for about 3 days now and he just now got out. Tonsellites [tonsillitis] he says was the cause. If there is a raincoat at home with my serial No. in it H-0600 write and tell me or I will be charged for a new one. Well so long for now and find out about the raincoat if you can
Your Loving Son, Bob.

full_transcription:
Dear Dad

I got your letter and the money. I haven't been down to Alvin lately because I have restricted but I am going down this week end if they we get off in time. Got another letter from Jimmy Saturday and he says he hasn't been warm for two days. It was warm here yesterday and today. We haven't wore nothing but shirt sleeve for a couple of days. All men in the Air Corps saw the Captain Sat. and he explains to us what was going on. All 18 yr. before Dec. 1 will receive a 14 day furlough if they wish to after this term is finished. You can go to basic right from here if you want to, but everybody wants a furlough. The govt. pays our way from our home to Leavenworth, but we have to pay our way home from here. If we leave for basic training from here they will pay our way there. I will make enough from Des Moines to Leavenworth to make up for my fare from Laramie to Des Moines. I called up Sunday about 7:15 and it took a few min to get thru and I am sorry I missed getting to talk to you. I hope Dean had a nice birthday. We have been playing basketball in P.T. lately and between platoon. Yesterday was the first day I seem to get the feel of the ball and I am made a few points. I have been feeling pretty good lately but the last few days my stomach has been about halfway upset but I still am walking. My roommate has been in the infirmary for about 3 days now and he just now got out. Tonsellites [tonsillitis] he says was the cause. If there is a raincoat at home with my serial No. in it H-0600 write and tell me or I will be charged for a new one. Well so long for now and find out about the raincoat if you can
Your Loving Son, Bob.
summary: Bob writes to his father from military training, likely at an Air Corps base near Alvin, Texas. He reports on furlough policies — soldiers under 18 before December 1 will receive a 14-day furlough after their current term — and discusses the travel pay arrangements between home, Des Moines, and Leavenworth. He mentions playing basketball in PT, a mild stomach upset, his roommate recovering from tonsillitis, and asks his father to check if a raincoat with his serial number H-0600 is at home, otherwise he'll be charged for a replacement.
